title: Rate reality hits home for older people in debt
full_text:
Interest rate rises are just not the same any more.
Once upon a time, they only hit the mortgage belt, that fabled housing strip in the suburbs populated by young families. Today, the RBA’s weapon of choice also lands heavily on older people.
The shift has been steady but irreversible. Houses are bought later in life, mortgages last longer and before you know it you are 55 with a mortgage that’s miles away from being paid off.
The latest rate increase takes the RBA official rate up to 4.35 per cent, from 4.1 per cent, in turn pushing home mortgages closer to 7 per cent.
Ray White chief economist Nerida Conisbee says the increase isn’t just hurting younger families.
“I think the idea that you get to retirement and have paid off your mortgage, for a lot of households this is quite foreign now,” Conisbee has told The Australian’s The Money Puzzle podcast.
“In previous generations, it was a very normal thing to have paid off your mortgage by your 50s perhaps.” Conisbee says the level of debt held by Australians also means the impact of interests rates on household budgets is more significant.
“The Reserve Bank doesn’t need to move much on interest rates because compared to the early ’80s people have just got much higher levels of debt,” Conisbee says.
“So even a small nudge upwards has quite an oversized impact on people with mortgages.” As more older Australians carry the interest rate burden, banks have been moving for years to service the indebted not by making loans cheaper, but instead through allowing those loans to last longer.
“Banks are really extending loan terms,” Conisbee says.
“If you have a look at CBA, it used to be a pretty standard to get a 25-year loan. It’s now 30 and every time they move it out it increases people’s borrowing capacity but it also then increases the time period in which they’re paying off a mortgage.” As the latest rate cut, the third so far this year, reverses all of the rate cuts last year, financial advisers are concerned about the cumulative effect of rising rates on older Australians still stuck with a mortgage.
“For clients closer to retirement, the calculus shifts considerably,” Southern Advisory financial adviser James Hayes says.
“When you’re 10 years out or less from stopping work, every dollar going out in mortgage interest is a dollar that isn’t compounding inside super. The maths on this is sobering when you actually run it.” Census data shows that over the past two decades the number of Australians aged 55 to 64 who owned their homes outright had almost halved.
For many older people, if the mortgage is ever to be cleared, then the key pathway is to pay the bank from superannuation savings just when they were about to retire in some comfort.
According to a Colonial First State report, about 14 per cent of Australians are retiring with a home loan still being serviced, while 28 per cent of pre-retirees between the ages of 50 and 64 still have a mortgage.
Eventually this sandwiched generation will fade from the scene, but they will not be fully replaced. That’s because fewer people in the future will aspire to be mortgaged homeowners.
A key report from the Australian Housing and Urban Research Institute forecast that the overall level of ownership is set to drop from a long-term average near 70 per cent to 63 per cent by 2040.
Some older people may see the upside of higher rates when the banks adjust cash deposit rates higher in the weeks ahead, but AMP chief economist Shane Oliver has pointed to the wider problem that the level of household debt in Australia is still almost double the size of the household bank deposits by value. For now, the squeeze is on the middle-aged, highly indebted mortgaged holders who have paid the price of buying a home in a market where prices remains elevated on every measure.
summary: Older Australians are increasingly burdened by mortgage debt as interest rates rise, with many approaching retirement still carrying home loans.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The RBA's interest rate increase to 4.35% is significantly impacting older Australians with mortgages.
The trend of older homeowners carrying debt is rising, with many needing to use superannuation to pay off mortgages.
Census data indicates a decline in outright home ownership among those aged 55 to 64, projected to continue into the future.
relevance: Understanding the financial pressures on older Australians is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in advising clients on retirement planning and debt management.
